name: Case Study - Vincenzo Albano ARPS | RPS Distinctions

step.name: Why did you want to gain a distinction with the Royal Photographic Society (RPS)?
step.text: After obtaining my RPS Licentiateship in 2002, I felt it was time, to sum up, my scattered photographic experience into a prestigious qualification.

step.name: Why did you choose Alan to mentor you for a distinction and had you worked with Alan before the RPS Mentoring Course?
step.text: I felt that my weakest area was editing, therefore I needed to learn how to manage a workflow that would “pop” my images, still retaining their original flavour and immediacy. Alan’s portfolio on his website shows how, carefully taken pictures, could be enhanced to an artistic level, still retaining their authenticity.

step.name: Describe how you found the RPS Mentoring Classes.
step.text: Alan has been pivotal in guiding me through editing, so important in digital photography. Having a strong and long-dated experience with film photography, I initially struggled to adapt to the new world of digital. Alan has shown me how savvy editing can transform a dull image into storytelling.

step.name: What distinction did you apply for and were you successful?
step.text: ARPS in Documentary Photography and yes, it was successful.

step.name: What was your experience like of the RPS Assessment Day?
Did you seek independent advice, feedback or input from anywhere else before your panel assessment?
step.text: I attended an RPS Advisory day and also benefitted from RPS online advice.

step.name: Having completed the course and Distinction Assessment, what advice would you like to share with others?
step.text: The standard required for RPS distinctions is very high. You need to demonstrate understanding and control of the three major steps: shooting, editing and printing. Storytelling is also an important part particularly at ARPS level, showing consistency between the Statement of Intent and the Portfolio. Nevertheless, I suppose that Licentiate applicants would also benefit by creating a “narrative” portfolio. Therefore, preparation in all the above fields is highly recommended. Budget is also important to prevent last-minute surprises. Lastly, presentation is so important: I would recommend us...

step.name: What was the hardest part of gaining a distinction with the RPS and why?
step.text: There isn’t a specific “hard” part, the entire process is demanding! If I have to pick one, consistency between intent and narrative requires lots of hard thinking.
